channel.title: Models & Agents for Beginners
channel.description: A daily AI podcast for beginners and teens. Learn about AI models, agents, and the tools shaping our future — explained simply, with hands-on experiments you can try today. Every expert started as a beginner.
channel.itunes:author: Patrick
channel.itunes:summary: A daily AI podcast for beginners and teens. Learn about AI models, agents, and the tools shaping our future — explained simply, with hands-on experiments you can try today. Every expert started as a beginner.

item.title: Ep 1: Imagine AI getting super smart by "watching" billions of videos, just like you binge TikToks to learn new trends.
item.description:
# Models & Agents for Beginners **Date:** March 08, 2026
**HOOK:** Imagine AI getting super smart by "watching" billions of videos, just like you binge TikToks to learn new trends.
**What's Cool Today:** Today's biggest buzz is how Meta's researchers are turning to unlabeled videos to train AI, since text data is running low — think of it as giving AI eyes to learn from the visual world, which could make tools like chatbots way better at understanding real-life stuff. We'll also compare two popular AI assistants you can try for free, and look at why AI tests are missing most real jobs. Stick around for simple explanations, fun try-this ideas, and why this all matters for your school projects or future gigs.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ ### The Big Story Researchers from Meta FAIR and New York University just trained a new kind of AI model from scratch using both text and images, and they discovered that some old ideas about building these models aren't as important as we thought. They focused on using tons of unlabeled video data — meaning videos without any captions or labels — as a huge new way to teach AI, especially since high-quality text for training is getting harder to find. This is like upgrading from reading books to watching movies to learn about the world; the AI doesn't need everything spelled out because it can pick up patterns from just seeing things happen in videos. Multimodal AI (models that handle different types of data like text, images, and video all at once) could learn more naturally this way, similar to how kids learn by observing without always needing explanations. This is a big deal because it might make AI better at everyday tasks, like helping with homework by understanding diagrams or videos, or even creating art inspired by real-world movements in games and social media. For students or career changers, it opens doors to AI that "sees" the world like we do, potentially revolutionizing fields like education, where AI could tutor using visual examples, or creative hobbies like editing TikToks with smarter suggestions. Teens might love how this could improve AI in apps for making music videos or analyzing sports clips for fun projects. If you're a parent or educator, this means future tools could make learning more interactive and less text-heavy. For you specifically, this could mean chatbots that get your video-based questions right, like "explain this dance move from a clip," without fumbling. While the exact model isn't public yet, you can explore similar ideas right now by heading to free tools like Google's Gemini or OpenAI's ChatGPT and uploading an image or describing a video scene — try asking "What patterns do you see in this photo of a busy street?" to see multimodal magic in action. Or, check out YouTube's AI features for video summaries and imagine how video training could supercharge them. It's an easy way to dip your toes into why visuals are the next big thing for AI. Source: https://the-decoder.com/llm-text-data-is-drying-up-but-meta-points-to-unlabeled-video-as-the-next-massive-training-frontier/
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ ### Explain Like I'm 14 Let's dive into how AI agents actually get tested and improved, a concept popping up in today's news about benchmarks — basically, these are like report cards for AI, scoring how well they handle tasks. Imagine you're training for a soccer team, but the coach only tests you on free kicks and ignores dribbling, passing, or defense; you'd end up great at one thing but clueless about the full game, right? That's similar to what's happening with AI agents (digital helpers that can plan and do tasks step by step, like an AI version of a personal assistant). Step one: Developers create benchmarks, which are sets of challenges, but most focus heavily on coding tasks, like writing a program — that's like only practicing those free kicks because they're easy to measure. Step two: The AI tries the tasks, and its score shows strengths, but if 92% of real jobs...
item.itunes:summary:
# Models & Agents for Beginners **Date:** March 08, 2026
**HOOK:** Imagine AI getting super smart by "watching" billions of videos, just like you binge TikToks to learn new trends.
**What's Cool Today:** Today's biggest buzz is how Meta's researchers are turning to unlabeled videos to train AI, since text data is running low — think of it as giving AI eyes to learn from the visual world, which could make tools like chatbots way better at understanding real-life stuff. We'll also compare two popular AI assistants you can try for free, and look at why AI tests are missing most real jobs. Stick around for simple explanations, fun try-this ideas, and why this all matters for your school projects or future gigs.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ ### The Big Story Researchers from Meta FAIR and New York University just trained a new kind of AI model from scratch using both text and images, and they discovered that some old ideas about building these models aren't as important as we thought. They focused on using tons of unlabeled video data — meaning videos without any captions or labels — as a huge new way to teach AI, especially since high-quality text for training is getting harder to find. This is like upgrading from reading books to watching movies to learn about the world; the AI doesn't need everything spelled out because it can pick up patterns from just seeing things happen in videos. Multimodal AI (models that handle different types of data like text, images, and video all at once) could learn more naturally this way, similar to how kids learn by observing without always needing explanations. This is a big deal because it might make AI better at everyday tasks, like helping with homework by understanding diagrams or videos, or even creating art inspired by real-world movements in games and social media. For students or career changers, it opens doors to AI that "sees" the world like we do, potentially revolutionizing fields like education, where AI could tutor using visual examples, or creative hobbies like editing TikToks with smarter suggestions. Teens might love how this could improve AI in apps for making music videos or analyzing sports clips for fun projects. If you're a parent or educator, this means future tools could make learning more interactive and less text-heavy. For you specifically, this could mean chatbots that get your video-based questions right, like "explain this dance move from a clip," without fumbling. While the exact model isn't public yet, you can explore similar ideas right now by heading to free tools like Google's Gemini or OpenAI's ChatGPT and uploading an image or describing a video scene — try asking "What patterns do you see in this photo of a busy street?" to see multimodal magic in action. Or, check out YouTube's AI features for video summaries and imagine how video training could supercharge them. It's an easy way to dip your toes into why visuals are the next big thing for AI. Source: https://the-decoder.com/llm-text-data-is-drying-up-but-meta-points-to-unlabeled-video-as-the-next-massive-training-frontier/
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ ### Explain Like I'm 14 Let's dive into how AI agents actually get tested and improved, a concept popping up in today's news about benchmarks — basically, these are like report cards for AI, scoring how well they handle tasks. Imagine you're training for a soccer team, but the coach only tests you on free kicks and ignores dribbling, passing, or defense; you'd end up great at one thing but clueless about the full game, right? That's similar to what's happening with AI agents (digital helpers that can plan and do tasks step by step, like an AI version of a personal assistant). Step one: Developers create benchmarks, which are sets of challenges, but most focus heavily on coding tasks, like writing a program — that's like only practicing those free kicks because they're easy to measure. Step two: The AI tries the tasks, and its score shows strengths, but if 92% of real jobs...
item.itunes:title: Ep 1: Imagine AI getting super smart by "watching" billions of videos, just like you binge TikToks to learn new trends.
